:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Admission Types

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. In Missouri in 2020, people in prison for new court commitments accounted for 75% of the prison population, while parole returns and revocations accounted for 25%.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Trends

The graph below provides a decade-long perspective on the prison system in Missouri, highlighting three key metrics from 2010 to 2020. It showcases the overall prison population trend, comparing it with the number of individuals who were released from prison to parole, as well as those who met the eligibility criteria for parole but remained incarcerated.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Who's in Prison?

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethinicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male individuals made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, individuals aged 25-34 years made up the largest portion of people in prison for both new court commitments and parole returns and revocations.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offense


``` ::: :::

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

Offenders convicted of class C or D felonies related to drugs, DWI, or other non- violent crimes are generally eligible for release after 15% of their sentence has been served. Offenders convicted of class A or B non-violent or drug felonies, or class B DWIs are eligible for release after 25% of their sentence has been served. If a parolee is serving consecutive sentences, the minimum term for eligibility for parole must be calculated by adding the minimum terms for each individual sentence together; however, this must not exceed the minimum term for parole eligibility for an ordinary life sentence. Several types of convictions make offenders ineligible for parole; these include convictions for first-degree murder, tampering with a victim or witness, certain recidivist drug trafficking offenses, and conviction as a persis- tent sexual offender. Circuit courts have jurisdiction to grant parole to offenders serving sentences under 2 1/2 years (in other words, those serving non-prison sentences). There do not appear to be guidelines or other criteria for court-based parole release.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 2,796 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes and with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 12% of the prison population.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 62% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 91% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated, comprising 36% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.


``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 5-9.9 years, accounting for 44% of this group.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to rape or sexual assault, accounting for 29% of this group.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Missing Data

This section will have information about missing parole eligibility data for the state.
``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Gender

(1) bar charts including community, prison pop, parole-eligible pop, paroled at first opportunity pop (2) RRIs for each of those populations, (3) release date compared to first eligibility date (e.g., 50% released first year, 22% year after first eligibility, 15% 2 years after eligible, etc.)

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Race and Ethnicity

For every White person in prison, there are 3.1 Black, non-Hispanic people in prison.
``` ::: :::
![](C:/Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/ggplots/rri_infograph_Georgia.png){ width=200px }
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Note: The Relative Rate Index (RRI) measures racial and ethnic disparities by comparing rates between groups, often using White individuals as the reference. The RRI provides insight into the degree of overrepresentation or underrepresentation, with an RRI greater than 1 indicating that a particular racial or ethnic group is disproportionately represented. Source: BJS Prisoners (2020), Census (2020)









``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date

``` ::: :::
